content:
THINKING:
To write effectively for a skeptical adult who distrusts the topic and thinks it may be overhyped or wrong, I need to keep the tone straightforward, factual, and respectful of their skepticism. Avoid jargon or overly technical language, but still be precise enough to convey the biological reality. I should begin by acknowledging their skepticism indirectly, to build trust, then quickly explain the role of mRNA clearly and simply. Using analogies can help make abstract concepts tangible—like calling mRNA a "messenger" or "instruction sheet" that cells use. I will focus on the core function: mRNA carries genetic instructions from DNA to the cell's protein-making machinery. I'll avoid suggesting exaggerated claims or promises, sticking strictly to what mRNA does inside the cell. The flow should be logical: what mRNA is, where it comes from, what it does, and why that matters. This will help the reader see the biological basis without hype.

===BEGIN===
You might be skeptical about how mRNA works, and that’s understandable. Simply put, mRNA is a kind of molecular messenger inside your cells. Your DNA holds the full set of instructions for everything your body needs, but it stays safely inside the cell’s nucleus. When the cell needs to make a specific protein—a building block for many functions—it copies the relevant instructions into a molecule called messenger RNA, or mRNA. This mRNA then leaves the nucleus and travels to a part of the cell called the ribosome, which reads the instructions and assembles the protein accordingly. Think of mRNA as a temporary instruction sheet, carrying a precise message from your genetic library to the protein factory. This process is a normal, ongoing part of how all cells work, not a new or exaggerated concept.
===END===
